planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring after last frost danger has passed in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8–9 mulch heavily in fall to protect the crown from freezing temperatures.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 8–11; cape plumbago is an outstanding flowering shrub for Gulf Coast landscapes, producing nearly perpetual clusters of sky-blue or white phlox-like flowers that butterflies and native bees find irresistible. In zone 8 the top growth is often killed by hard frosts but the roots re-emerge vigorously in spring; in zone 9–11 it is essentially evergreen and can grow to 6 feet or more, where it works well as an informal hedge or sprawling border shrub. Prune hard in late winter before new growth begins to encourage dense, compact form — cape plumbago blooms on new wood, so pruning never reduces flowering. Very drought-tolerant once established; overwatering in heavy clay soil is the most common cause of root problems.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds after last frost or plant bare-root transplants in full to partial sun in well-drained soil on a fence or trellis; maypop spreads by underground runners and a sturdy support is needed for the vigorous vines.

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant transplants in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil against a trellis, fence, or arbor; maypop is one of the most ornate native vines of the Southeast, producing intricately structured lavender-and-white flowers all summer followed by fragrant, egg-sized, yellow-green edible fruits that ripen in fall and make excellent juice and jam with a tropical guava-like flavor. The vine spreads vigorously by underground runners and can colonize an area quickly — provide a sturdy trellis and monitor spread, or allow it to naturalize along a fence row. Maypop is the sole or primary host plant for Gulf Fritillary and Zebra Longwing butterfly larvae in the Southeast, making it one of the most ecologically valuable native plants for zone 5–9 pollinator gardens. Despite top growth dying back in zone 5–7, the deep roots re-emerge dependably each spring.

planting.note: Purchase rooted slips or root cuttings from an overwintered tuber; transplant only after last frost when soil is warm, in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil — morning sun with afternoon shade extends the display in hot climates.

directions: Purchase rooted slips or start cuttings from overwintered tubers and transplant after last frost in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil; ornamental sweet potato vine is one of the most dramatic foliage plants for containers, hanging baskets, and ground cover use in warm-season gardens, producing an exuberant cascade of heart-shaped to deeply lobed leaves in purple-black, chartreuse, bronze, or tricolor combinations depending on cultivar. Growth is vigorous in summer heat, easily spreading 4–6 feet per season to fill containers and cascade elegantly over walls. The small tubers that form underground are edible but bland compared to culinary sweet potato varieties; the ornamental appeal is entirely the foliage. In zone 9–11 leave tubers in the ground year-round as a perennial; elsewhere dig tubers after the first light frost and store overwinter in a cool, dry location.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; cordyline tolerates wind, coastal salt spray, and brief frosts to about 14°F, making it well suited to zone 8–11 coastal and subtropical landscapes.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; cordyline creates striking architectural focal points with its long sword-like leaves radiating from a central trunk in green, burgundy, bronze, or pink depending on cultivar. Tolerant of wind, coastal salt spray, and brief frosts down to about 14°F, it thrives in zone 8–11 landscapes where it adds unmistakable tropical structure as a specimen, backdrop, or container plant. Growth is moderately fast in warm climates where it can eventually reach 15–20 feet; strip older lower leaves as they dry to maintain the clean, palm-like trunk. Water regularly until established, then reduce to occasional deep watering as it is quite drought-tolerant once roots are set.

planting.note: Root tip cuttings or 'log' sections in moist potting mix or water at 70–80°F; in zone 10–12 plant outdoors in partial shade in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained fertile soil. Grow as a container plant in colder climates, bringing indoors before frost.

directions: Root tip cuttings or stem sections ('logs') in moist potting mix or water at 70–80°F, then plant outdoors in partial shade in moist, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile, well-drained soil in zone 10–12; the ti plant has been a sacred and culturally significant plant in Pacific Island and Hawaiian cultures for millennia, prized for its deep burgundy, pink, green, or tricolor strap leaves and grown outdoors year-round in tropical gardens. Bright filtered shade deepens the leaf coloration — full sun bleaches the colors and deep shade reduces vibrancy and causes leggy growth. Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged and fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season. Outside zone 10–12, grow as a houseplant or deck container in the highest indirect light available and bring indoors before the first frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to moderately wet, fertile, well-drained to poorly drained soil in partial shade to full sun; crinum thrives in seasonally flooded areas, pond margins, and the wet spots that defeat most other bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to wet, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; crinum lily is one of the most heat-tolerant and moisture-tolerant bulbs available for zone 7–11 gardens, thriving in conditions from boggy pond margins to regularly inundated lawn edges where most ornamentals fail. The intensely fragrant white spider-like flowers appear on tall scapes above the broad, strap-like, semi-evergreen foliage in summer and attract sphinx moths and swallowtail butterflies at dusk. Clumps multiply steadily by offsets and require little care beyond dividing every 5–7 years when they become congested. Hardy to approximately 15°F with heavy mulch in zone 7; essentially evergreen in zone 9–11 coastal Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring after last frost in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil in zone 8–11; Mexican petunia spreads by both rhizomes and self-seeding — choose sterile cultivars such as 'Chi Chi' or 'Purple Showers' near natural areas or water bodies.

directions: Plant container transplants or rooted cuttings in spring after last frost in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil in zone 8–11; Mexican petunia blooms nearly continuously from spring through frost with tubular purple, pink, or white flowers that hummingbirds and swallowtails visit throughout the day. Established plants are highly drought-tolerant and thrive in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers with minimal care. The species spreads aggressively by rhizomes and by explosive seed dispersal and is listed as invasive in Florida — select non-seeding sterile cultivars ('Chi Chi', 'Purple Showers', 'Mayan Purple') when planting near natural areas or waterways. In zone 8 the top growth is killed by frost but the roots re-emerge reliably each spring; cut back to the ground in late winter to remove dead stems.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct-sow outdoors after soil warms to 70°F in full sun in well-drained, lean, alkaline-tolerant soil; yellow bells grows rapidly and may bloom in its first season from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, alkaline-tolerant soil after last frost; yellow bells (esperanza) is one of the most floriferous and heat-tolerant shrubs for Gulf Coast and Southwest desert gardens, producing masses of bright golden-yellow trumpet flowers from early summer through the first hard frost that hummingbirds find irresistible. Growth is rapid — plants can reach 6–8 feet in a single season in warm climates — and the pinnately compound foliage is attractive throughout the growing season. In zone 8 the top growth is killed back in hard winters but roots re-emerge vigorously; in zone 9–11 it is a semi-evergreen large shrub or small tree reaching 8–12 feet. Prune hard in late winter or early spring to maintain compact form and maximize the season's flower production.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to wet, well-drained to moderately drained fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; hymenocallis thrives near water features, pond margins, and in moist low-lying areas of zone 7–11 gardens.

directions: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun near water features, pond edges, or in moist low spots; the spider lily produces one of the most structurally distinctive flowers in the summer garden — a white central cup is surrounded by six long, narrow, arching white petals that give each flower an elegant spider-like form, and the intensely sweet perfume is released especially in the evening hours. The broad, strap-like, semi-evergreen foliage remains attractive throughout the year in zone 9–11. Established clumps multiply by offsets and require minimal care; divide every 4–5 years in late fall after the foliage dies back when clumps become crowded. In zone 7–8, lift bulbs after the first frost and store in a cool, dry location through winter.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after last frost in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; Mexican bush sage does not bloom reliably from seed — propagate by stem cuttings taken in spring or early summer.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in zone 8–11 after last frost; Mexican bush sage is one of the showiest late-season salvias, producing arching purple-and-white or all-purple velvety flower spikes from late summer through frost that are among the most effective hummingbird and butterfly plants available in the South. The plant grows quickly to form a large, rounded shrub with soft, grey-green foliage that may look coarse early in the season but becomes spectacular from September onward. Cut to the ground in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges; the root is winter-hardy in zone 8–11 even when top growth is frost-killed. Propagate by stem cuttings rather than seed, as seed-grown plants bloom much later and less reliably.
